contents.value:
```powershell
Get-Process |
    Where-Object { $_.CPU -gt 10 } |
    Sort-Object CPU -Descending |
    Select-Object -First 5 Name, CPU
```
**Pipeline** passes full .NET objects between commands (not just text like Unix pipes). Each `|` feeds output to the next command's input.

```powershell
$a -eq $b      # equal
$a -ne $b      # not equal
$a -gt $b      # greater than
$a -lt $b      # less than
$a -like '*.txt'   # wildcard match
$a -match '^[0-9]' # regex match
$a -contains $b    # collection contains
```
**Comparison operators** - PowerShell uses `-eq`, `-gt`, etc. instead of `==`, `>`. Case-insensitive by default; add `c` prefix for case-sensitive (`-ceq`).

```powershell
$name = "World"          # string
$count = 42              # integer
$items = @(1, 2, 3)     # array
$hash = @{ a = 1 }      # hashtable
$env:PATH                # environment variable
$_                       # current pipeline object
```
**Variables** start with `$`. PowerShell is dynamically typed. `$env:` accesses environment variables.

```powershell
Get-Service | Where-Object Status -eq 'Running'
Get-Service -Name 'nginx'
Restart-Service -Name 'nginx' -Force
Stop-Service -Name 'IISAdmin'
```
**Service cmdlets** manage Windows services. Get, Start, Stop, Restart, Set, and New are available.

```powershell
$params = @{
    Path = 'C:\logs'
    Filter = '*.log'
    Recurse = $true
}
Get-ChildItem @params
```
**Splatting** passes a hashtable of parameters using `@` instead of `$`. Cleaner than long single-line commands.
